meta.title: ML Engineering
meta.description: Review checklist for ML Engineering projects

items.text: Are input data schemas validated before ingestion?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every data pipeline should enforce schema contracts (column names, types, nullability) on incoming data before any transformation or model training begins.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Silent schema changes in upstream data sources are one of the most common causes of model degradation. A missing column or type change can produce garbage predictions without any error.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that the pipeline validates schemas at the entry point
- Look for assertions on column presence, data types, and value ranges
- Confirm that schema violations raise loud errors rather than silently dropping rows
baseContent.exampleComment: This pipeline reads the CSV directly into the training step. Add a schema validation step (e.g., with Pandera or Great Expectations) before feature extraction.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
df = pd.read_csv("events.csv")
features = extract_features(df)  # no validation

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
import pandera as pa

schema = pa.DataFrameSchema({
    "user_id": pa.Column(int, nullable=False),
    "event_type": pa.Column(str, pa.Check.isin(["click", "view", "purchase"])),
    "timestamp": pa.Column(pa.DateTime),
})

df = pd.read_csv("events.csv")
schema.validate(df)
features = extract_features(df)

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Validate data schemas at pipeline boundaries. Fail fast on unexpected data rather than training on garbage.

items.text: Is the same feature transformation code used in both training and serving?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Training/serving skew occurs when the feature engineering code differs between the training pipeline and the serving path, causing the model to see different input distributions at inference time.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Even subtle differences in preprocessing (e.g., different normalization, tokenization, or imputation logic) can silently degrade model accuracy in production without triggering any error.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check if feature transformations are defined once and shared between training and serving
- Look for duplicated preprocessing logic in separate training and serving codebases
- Verify that serialized transformers (scalers, encoders) from training are loaded at serving time
baseContent.exampleComment: The serving endpoint re-implements the normalization inline instead of loading the fitted scaler from training. This will cause train/serve skew if the mean or std changes.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
# training.py
scaler = StandardScaler().fit(X_train)
X_scaled = scaler.transform(X_train)

# serving.py — reimplements normalization
X_scaled = (X - 0.5) / 0.3  # hardcoded values

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
# training.py
scaler = StandardScaler().fit(X_train)
joblib.dump(scaler, "scaler.pkl")

# serving.py — reuses the same scaler
scaler = joblib.load("scaler.pkl")
X_scaled = scaler.transform(X)

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Define feature transformations once and share them between training and serving to eliminate skew.

items.text: Is there any data leakage between training, validation, and test sets?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Data leakage happens when information from the validation or test set bleeds into the training process, giving an artificially inflated evaluation metric.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A model with data leakage will appear to perform well in offline evaluation but fail in production because it memorized information it should not have had access to.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that train/val/test splits happen before any preprocessing or feature engineering
- Verify that scalers and encoders are fit only on training data
- Look for temporal leakage in time-series data where future information leaks into past samples
baseContent.exampleComment: The StandardScaler is fit on the full dataset before splitting. Fit it only on X_train, then transform X_val and X_test separately.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
scaler = StandardScaler().fit(X)  # fit on ALL data
X_scaled = scaler.transform(X)
X_train, X_test = train_test_split(X_scaled)

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
X_train, X_test = train_test_split(X)
scaler = StandardScaler().fit(X_train)  # fit on train ONLY
X_train = scaler.transform(X_train)
X_test = scaler.transform(X_test)

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always split before preprocessing. Fit transformers on training data only.

items.text: Are null and missing values handled explicitly in the pipeline?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The pipeline should have explicit strategies for dealing with missing values rather than silently dropping rows or letting NaNs propagate through calculations.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unhandled nulls can cause silent NaN propagation in numeric computations, biased models from dropped rows, or runtime errors in production serving.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check for explicit null handling (imputation, sentinel values, or documented dropping)
- Look for .dropna() calls without justification
- Verify that the serving path handles nulls the same way as training
baseContent.exampleComment: This pipeline calls dropna() without logging how many rows are dropped. Add explicit imputation or at least log the null rate so drift can be monitored.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Handle missing values explicitly and consistently between training and serving.

items.text: Is the data pipeline idempotent when re-run on the same inputs?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Running the pipeline twice on the same input data should produce the same output without duplicating records or corrupting state.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Non-idempotent pipelines cause duplicate data, inflated metrics, and inconsistent training datasets when retried after failures.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check for upsert logic or deduplication in write steps
- Look for unique constraints or partition overwriting in the output sink
- Verify that pipeline reruns do not append duplicate rows
baseContent.exampleComment: The pipeline appends to the output table on every run. Use MERGE/upsert or overwrite the partition to make this idempotent.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Design pipelines to be safely re-runnable. Use upserts or partition overwrites instead of blind appends.

items.text: Are random seeds set for reproducibility?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Random seeds should be fixed in all sources of randomness (NumPy, Python random, framework-specific seeds) so that experiments can be exactly reproduced.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without fixed seeds, you cannot reproduce a result to verify it, debug it, or compare it fairly against another experiment.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check for seed-setting calls at the top of training scripts
- Verify seeds are set for all libraries (numpy, random, torch/tf)
- Confirm that data loading order is also deterministic
baseContent.exampleComment: This training script does not set random seeds. Add seed initialization for numpy, random, and the framework to ensure reproducibility.

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
import random
import numpy as np

SEED = 42
random.seed(SEED)
np.random.seed(SEED)

model = RandomForestClassifier(random_state=SEED)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Set seeds everywhere. Reproducible experiments are the foundation of reliable ML engineering.

items.text: Are hyperparameters configurable rather than hardcoded?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Hyperparameters like learning rate, batch size, and layer sizes should be passed via configuration rather than buried in code.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Hardcoded hyperparameters make it difficult to run experiments, compare configurations, and track what changed between model versions.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Look for magic numbers in model definitions and training loops
- Check that hyperparameters are loaded from config files, CLI args, or experiment tracking tools
- Verify that the experiment tracker logs the actual hyperparameter values used
baseContent.exampleComment: The learning rate 0.001 and batch size 32 are hardcoded in the training loop. Move these to a config file or argparse so they can be varied across experiments.

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
config = load_config("experiment.yaml")
optimizer = Adam(lr=config.learning_rate)
train_loader = DataLoader(dataset, batch_size=config.batch_size)

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Externalize hyperparameters so experiments are configurable and reproducible.

items.text: Is gradient clipping applied to prevent exploding gradients?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Gradient clipping caps the magnitude of gradients during backpropagation to prevent them from growing uncontrollably large.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Exploding gradients cause NaN losses, diverging training runs, and wasted compute. This is especially common in RNNs and deep transformers.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check for gradient clipping in the training loop
- Look for torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_ or tf.clip_by_global_norm
- Verify the clipping threshold is reasonable (typically 1.0-5.0)
baseContent.exampleComment: This training loop has no gradient clipping. Add clip_grad_norm_ to prevent exploding gradients, especially since this is a transformer model.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Clip gradients to keep training stable, especially for deep or recurrent architectures.

items.text: Are numerical operations guarded against overflow and underflow?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Operations like log, exp, and division should include epsilon guards or use numerically stable library functions to prevent inf/NaN values.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Numerical instability produces NaN losses that are hard to debug. It often appears only on certain data distributions, making it a production landmine.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Look for raw log() or exp() calls without clamping
- Check for division operations without epsilon in the denominator
- Verify that loss functions use numerically stable implementations (e.g., log_softmax instead of log(softmax))
baseContent.exampleComment: Using log(softmax(x)) is numerically unstable. Replace with F.log_softmax(x) or F.cross_entropy which fuses the operations for stability.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use numerically stable library functions. Never compute log(softmax(x)) in two steps.

items.text: Is the model serialized in a portable, versioned format?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Trained models should be saved in a format that can be loaded independently of the training code version, with clear versioning metadata.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Pickle-based serialization is fragile and breaks when code is refactored. Without versioning, you cannot roll back to a previous model or audit what was deployed.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check the serialization format (ONNX, SavedModel, TorchScript are preferred over raw pickle)
- Verify that model artifacts include version metadata
- Confirm that the model can be loaded without the training codebase
baseContent.exampleComment: Saving the model with pickle ties it to the exact class definition. Consider using ONNX or TorchScript for portability, and include a version tag in the artifact.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Serialize models in portable formats with version metadata for safe deployment and rollback.

items.text: Is the model set to evaluation mode during inference?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Frameworks like PyTorch use different behavior for layers like Dropout and BatchNorm during training vs. inference. The model must be set to eval mode before serving predictions.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Forgetting model.eval() means dropout is still active during inference, producing non-deterministic and degraded predictions in production.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that model.eval() is called before inference
- Look for torch.no_grad() context manager around inference code
- Verify that batch normalization layers use running statistics, not batch statistics
baseContent.exampleComment: The serving function does not call model.eval(). This means dropout layers are still active, producing noisy predictions.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
model = load_model("checkpoint.pt")
prediction = model(input_tensor)  # still in train mode

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
model = load_model("checkpoint.pt")
model.eval()
with torch.no_grad():
    prediction = model(input_tensor)

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always call model.eval() and use torch.no_grad() for inference.

items.text: Are all relevant metrics logged during training?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Training runs should log loss curves, evaluation metrics, learning rate schedules, and other diagnostic information to an experiment tracker.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without logged metrics, you cannot compare experiments, diagnose training issues, or justify model selection decisions to stakeholders.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that training and validation loss are logged at each epoch
- Verify that business-relevant metrics (precision, recall, AUC) are logged
- Confirm that metrics are sent to a tracking tool (MLflow, W&B, etc.) not just printed to stdout
baseContent.exampleComment: The training loop only prints loss to stdout. Log metrics to MLflow or W&B so they are persisted and comparable across runs.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Log metrics to a persistent experiment tracker. Print statements are not experiment tracking.

items.text: Are model artifacts and datasets versioned together?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each trained model should be linked to the exact dataset version, code commit, and hyperparameters that produced it.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without artifact-dataset linking, you cannot reproduce a result, audit a deployed model, or trace a prediction back to its training data.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that the experiment tracker logs the dataset version or hash
- Verify that the git commit hash is recorded with each run
- Confirm that model artifacts are stored in a registry (MLflow Model Registry, S3 with versioning)
baseContent.exampleComment: This run does not record the dataset version. Add the dataset hash or DVC version to the experiment metadata.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Link every model to its exact dataset, code, and config. Full lineage enables auditing and reproduction.

items.text: Is every experiment compared against a documented baseline?

baseContent.whatItMeans: New model iterations should always be compared against a known baseline, whether that is the current production model, a simple heuristic, or a previous experiment.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without a baseline, you cannot tell whether a new model is actually better. A 90% accuracy means nothing if a simple rule achieves 89%.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that the PR or experiment report includes baseline metrics
- Verify that the comparison uses the same test set and evaluation protocol
- Look for statistical significance tests or confidence intervals for close results
baseContent.exampleComment: The experiment report shows 0.85 F1 but does not compare against the current production model. Add baseline metrics to demonstrate improvement.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always compare against a baseline. Absolute metrics without context are meaningless.

items.text: Do experiments follow a consistent naming convention?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Experiment runs should be named with a consistent pattern that encodes the key variables (model type, dataset, date) for easy filtering and search.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Inconsistent naming makes it hard to find and compare experiments, especially as the number of runs grows into the hundreds.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that experiment names follow a team convention
- Verify that names encode enough information to distinguish runs
- Look for auto-generated names that have been left as defaults
baseContent.exampleComment: This run is named 'test_run_3'. Use a descriptive name like 'resnet50-imagenet-lr0.01-20240115' that encodes the model, dataset, and key hyperparameters.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use descriptive, consistent experiment names. Your future self will thank you.

items.text: Are failed experiments documented with reasons for failure?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Experiments that did not improve metrics should be logged with notes explaining what was tried and why it did not work.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Undocumented failures lead to repeated work. Teams waste time re-running experiments that have already been shown to fail.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that the experiment tracker contains notes on runs with poor results
- Verify that the PR description explains what approaches were tried and rejected
- Look for a shared experiment log or knowledge base
baseContent.exampleComment: Good practice to log why this approach did not improve metrics. Add a note to the experiment run so the team does not revisit this dead end.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Document negative results. Knowing what does not work is as valuable as knowing what does.

items.text: Does the feature retrieval use point-in-time correct joins?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When joining features for training, the join must respect temporal ordering so that each training example only sees feature values that were available at prediction time.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without point-in-time correctness, future feature values leak into past training examples, giving unrealistically good offline metrics that do not hold in production.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that feature joins use timestamp-aware logic (as-of joins)
- Verify that the feature store API enforces point-in-time semantics
- Look for naive SQL joins on entity ID without a timestamp condition
baseContent.exampleComment: This join fetches the latest feature value regardless of the event timestamp. Use an as-of join to get the feature value that was current at the time of each event.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
SELECT e.*, f.feature_value
FROM events e
JOIN features f ON e.user_id = f.user_id
-- gets the latest value, not the value at event time

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
SELECT e.*, f.feature_value
FROM events e
JOIN features f ON e.user_id = f.user_id
  AND f.timestamp <= e.timestamp
QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
  PARTITION BY e.event_id ORDER BY f.timestamp DESC
) = 1

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always use point-in-time correct joins. Temporal leakage is the most dangerous kind of data leakage.

items.text: Are features documented with descriptions, owners, and data sources?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each feature in the store should have a human-readable description, a designated owner, and a clear lineage back to its raw data source.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Undocumented features become tribal knowledge. New team members cannot discover or trust features they do not understand, leading to duplicated work.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that feature definitions include description fields
- Verify that an owner or team is assigned to each feature group
- Look for lineage metadata linking features to source tables
baseContent.exampleComment: This feature 'user_v3_agg' has no description. Add a human-readable description and document the aggregation window and source table.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Document features with descriptions, owners, and lineage. Features are shared assets that outlive their creators.

items.text: Are online and offline feature stores consistent?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The offline store (used for training) and online store (used for serving) should produce the same feature values for the same entity and timestamp.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Inconsistency between online and offline stores is a form of training/serving skew that degrades model performance in production without any code change.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that feature materialization populates both online and offline stores from the same source
- Look for separate pipelines that could drift
- Verify that consistency checks or integration tests compare online vs. offline values
baseContent.exampleComment: The online store is populated by a separate pipeline with different aggregation logic. Unify the pipelines or add consistency validation between stores.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Unify online and offline feature pipelines. Separate pipelines are a ticking skew bomb.

items.text: Is feature freshness monitored and appropriate for the use case?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Features should be updated frequently enough for the model's needs, and staleness should be monitored with alerts when features fall behind their expected refresh cadence.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Stale features silently degrade model predictions. A fraud model using 24-hour-old transaction counts will miss real-time fraud patterns.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that feature pipelines have SLAs for freshness
- Verify that monitoring alerts fire when features are stale
- Confirm that the refresh cadence matches the model's latency requirements
baseContent.exampleComment: This real-time fraud model uses features that are only refreshed daily. Either increase the refresh frequency or add a freshness check that falls back to a safe default.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Monitor feature freshness. Stale features are a silent model killer.

items.text: Are features designed for reuse across multiple models?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Feature engineering should produce general-purpose features that can be shared across models, rather than tightly coupling features to a single model.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Reusable features reduce duplicated computation, ensure consistency across models, and accelerate development of new models.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that features are named generically (not model-specific)
- Verify that feature definitions are in a shared registry rather than inline in training code
- Look for opportunities to extract common features from model-specific code
baseContent.exampleComment: This 'churn_model_user_activity_30d' feature is the same as the one used in the recommendation model. Rename it to 'user_activity_30d' and register it in the shared feature store.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Design features for the entity, not the model. Reusable features compound in value.

items.text: Is the new model deployed in shadow mode before serving live traffic?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Shadow mode means the new model runs alongside the current production model, receiving the same inputs and logging predictions, but its outputs are not served to users.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Shadow deployment catches performance issues, latency regressions, and unexpected prediction distributions before they affect real users.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check the deployment plan for a shadow or canary phase
- Verify that shadow predictions are logged for comparison
- Confirm that there is a go/no-go checklist before promoting to live traffic
baseContent.exampleComment: This PR deploys the new model directly to 100% of traffic. Add a shadow deployment phase to compare predictions against the current model before switching over.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Deploy new models in shadow mode first. Measure before you serve.

items.text: Is there a documented rollback plan to the previous model version?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The deployment should include a clear, tested plan to revert to the previous model version if the new model causes issues in production.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without a rollback plan, a bad model deployment can cause extended outages. ML model issues are often subtle and may not be caught by standard health checks.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that the previous model version is preserved and loadable
- Verify that the rollback procedure is documented and has been tested
- Confirm that rollback can be done without a code deploy (e.g., config change or model registry update)
baseContent.exampleComment: The deployment doc does not mention rollback. Document how to revert to the previous model version and verify the procedure works in staging.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always have a tested rollback plan. The best deployment safety net is a fast revert.

items.text: Does the model meet the latency budget for its serving context?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The model inference time (including preprocessing) must fit within the overall latency budget of the serving application, typically measured at p50, p95, and p99.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A model that is too slow for its serving context will cause timeouts, degraded user experience, or force the application to fall back to a simpler heuristic.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that inference latency has been benchmarked on production-like hardware
- Verify that p99 latency fits within the application's SLA
- Look for batch size and hardware requirements in the deployment plan
baseContent.exampleComment: The model takes 200ms per inference but the API has a 100ms SLA. Profile the model to find optimization opportunities (quantization, pruning, distillation).
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Benchmark inference latency on production hardware. A model that misses its latency budget is not deployable.

items.text: Is the A/B test properly configured with correct randomization?

baseContent.whatItMeans: A/B tests should use proper randomization at the correct unit (e.g., user-level, not request-level) with pre-registered success metrics and a sample size calculation.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Incorrect randomization (e.g., request-level instead of user-level) causes data leakage between variants. Missing sample size calculations lead to underpowered tests or premature conclusions.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that randomization is at the user level (or appropriate unit)
- Verify that success metrics are defined before the test starts
- Confirm that sample size and duration calculations are documented
baseContent.exampleComment: This A/B test randomizes at the request level, so the same user can see both variants. Randomize at the user level to avoid contamination between groups.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Randomize at the right unit and pre-register metrics. Poorly run A/B tests produce misleading results.

items.text: Are GPU/CPU/memory requirements specified for the serving infrastructure?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The deployment should specify the hardware requirements (GPU type, CPU cores, memory) needed to serve the model at the expected throughput.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Under-provisioned infrastructure causes latency spikes and OOM errors. Over-provisioned infrastructure wastes money. Both are avoidable with proper resource planning.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that the deployment config specifies resource requests and limits
- Verify that memory requirements account for model size plus inference overhead
- Confirm that GPU requirements match the model's compute needs
baseContent.exampleComment: The deployment does not specify memory limits. This 2GB model will need at least 4GB of memory when accounting for inference overhead. Add resource requests to the deployment config.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Specify resource requirements. Right-sizing infrastructure saves money and prevents outages.

items.text: Is the prediction distribution monitored for drift?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The distribution of model predictions (scores, classes) should be monitored over time to detect shifts that indicate model degradation or data drift.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Prediction drift is often the earliest signal that something has changed in the input data or the model is stale. Catching it early prevents extended periods of poor predictions.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that prediction distributions are logged and visualized
- Verify that statistical drift tests (KS test, PSI) are configured
- Confirm that alerts fire when drift exceeds thresholds
baseContent.exampleComment: The serving endpoint logs predictions but does not monitor their distribution. Add a drift detection check (e.g., PSI) that alerts when the prediction distribution shifts significantly.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Monitor prediction distributions. Drift detection is your early warning system.

items.text: Are input feature distributions monitored for drift?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The distribution of input features at serving time should be compared against the training distribution to detect data drift before it degrades predictions.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Input drift precedes prediction drift. Monitoring inputs lets you act before the model's predictions degrade, rather than waiting for downstream metric drops.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that feature statistics (mean, variance, cardinality) are logged during serving
- Verify that these are compared against training-time statistics
- Confirm that alerts fire on significant distribution shifts
baseContent.exampleComment: Feature distributions are not monitored in serving. Add statistical checks comparing serving-time feature distributions against training baselines.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Monitor input features for drift. Catching upstream data changes early prevents downstream model failures.

items.text: Is there a pipeline to collect ground truth labels for monitoring?

baseContent.whatItMeans: A feedback loop should collect actual outcomes (ground truth) and compare them against model predictions to compute real-world accuracy metrics.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without ground truth feedback, you can only monitor proxy metrics. The model could be making confidently wrong predictions that only surface when users complain.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that there is a mechanism to collect labels after predictions are made
- Verify that ground truth is joined back to predictions for accuracy computation
- Confirm that real-world metrics are tracked on a dashboard
baseContent.exampleComment: The model serves predictions but there is no pipeline to compare against actual outcomes. Set up a ground truth collection pipeline to compute real-world accuracy.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Close the feedback loop. Ground truth labels are the only way to measure real-world model performance.

items.text: Are error rate and timeout alerts configured for the serving endpoint?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The model serving endpoint should have alerts on error rates (5xx responses, inference failures) and latency timeouts to catch operational issues quickly.
baseContent.whyItMatters: ML serving failures are often silent. A model returning fallback predictions due to errors may not trigger standard application monitoring.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that error rate thresholds are configured in the monitoring system
- Verify that timeout alerts match the application's SLA
- Confirm that alerts page the right on-call team
baseContent.exampleComment: The serving endpoint has no alerting configured. Add error rate and p99 latency alerts so the ML team is paged when the model is unhealthy.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Alert on error rates and latency. Standard application monitoring is not enough for ML serving.

items.text: Are fairness metrics monitored across demographic groups?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Model performance should be disaggregated across relevant demographic or sensitive groups to detect disparate impact or performance disparities.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Aggregate metrics can hide significant performance gaps between groups. A model with 95% overall accuracy might have 70% accuracy for a minority group.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that evaluation metrics are broken down by relevant groups
- Verify that fairness metrics (demographic parity, equalized odds) are computed
- Confirm that disparities trigger review processes, not just alerts
baseContent.exampleComment: The evaluation report only shows aggregate accuracy. Break down metrics by the relevant demographic groups to ensure the model performs fairly across all users.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Disaggregate metrics across groups. Fairness cannot be assessed from aggregate numbers alone.

items.text: Is there an alert for when the model has not been retrained within its expected cadence?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Models should be retrained on a regular cadence appropriate to the domain. An alert should fire if retraining has not occurred within the expected window.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Stale models silently degrade as the world changes. Without a staleness alert, a broken retraining pipeline can go unnoticed for weeks.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that the model metadata includes a training timestamp
- Verify that an alert fires if the model age exceeds the retraining SLA
- Confirm that the retraining pipeline has health monitoring
baseContent.exampleComment: This model was last trained 90 days ago but the team's SLA is biweekly retraining. Add a staleness alert that fires when the model age exceeds 14 days.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Alert on model staleness. A broken retraining pipeline should not go unnoticed.
